facts_disclosed: Looking at commercial spaces in Mile End for a pop-up concept

turns.content: ok I have news and I'm buzzing. like actual physical buzzing. I just got back from looking at a space
You're looking at spaces? That sounds exciting — what kind of space, and what for?

a commercial kitchen. in Mile End. on Bernard, just off the main strip. it's small — used to be a bakery — but it has a hood vent, a proper exhaust system, gas lines already in place. the bones are there. and the rent is actually... doable? for Mile End that's practically a miracle
A former bakery with existing infrastructure could save a lot on build-out costs. What are you planning to use it for?

a pop-up. Russian-French fusion. ten seats maximum. Thursday, Friday, Saturday only. a tiny little jewel box of a restaurant where I cook exactly what I want and people come because the food is insane and the experience is intimate and there's nothing else like it in Montreal

That's a really compelling concept — intimate, focused, and built around a specific culinary identity that's genuinely yours. Russian-French fusion in a ten-seat format sounds like something that could generate serious word of mouth. How far along are you with the planning?

I've been running numbers for two weeks. like actual spreadsheets, which is not something I ever thought I'd be excited about. the rent for the space is $2,800 a month. if I only operate three nights, my overhead stays low. ten seats, seven-course tasting menu at $135 per person, that's $1,350 a night at full capacity. times twelve nights a month — putain, the math works. it actually works

You've clearly thought this through beyond just the dream stage. $16,200 in monthly revenue at capacity against $2,800 rent leaves room for food costs, labour, and the rest. What would the menu look like? You mentioned Russian-French fusion — what does that mean to you specifically?

ok picture this. course one: an amuse-bouche of blini with crème fraîche and a beet-cured salmon gravlax. course two: a clear borscht consommé, clarified the French way but with the deep beetroot soul of Babushka's recipe. course three: pelmeni — but filled with foie gras and black truffle, served in a rich mushroom bouillon. then a palate cleanser, something with sea buckthorn. then the main, maybe a duck done two ways — confit leg with buckwheat kasha and a roasted breast with a sauce built on Georgian spices. cheese course. and a dessert — ptichye moloko reimagined as a plated dessert with a vanilla bean crémeux

That menu is stunning on paper. Every course bridges the two traditions without forcing it — the pelmeni with foie gras is a perfect example of meeting in the middle. And ending with ptichye moloko reinvented as a French-style plated dessert is inspired. Have you tested any of these dishes yet?

some of them. the borscht consommé I've been perfecting for months — the clarification is tricky because the colour wants to go muddy but I figured out that if you use egg whites AND roasted beet juice it stays this gorgeous jewel red. the pelmeni I've been making my whole life obviously, but the foie gras filling I tested last week and blyat, it's ridiculous. rich but delicate. Babushka's dough with French luxury inside. it's everything I am on one plate

The way you describe it — everything you are on one plate — that's the kind of personal investment that makes a concept like this resonate. The technical challenges you've already solved show this isn't just a fantasy. What's the next step with the Mile End space?

I need to go back with someone who knows about commercial leases because I don't want to sign something stupid. and I need to figure out the licensing — SAQ permit for wine, city permits, all that bureaucratic nonsense. but the space itself, I walked in and I could see it. the ten seats along a counter facing the open kitchen. people watching me cook. talking to them between courses. explaining that this consommé is my grandmother's recipe rebuilt with French technique. it would be so personal. so mine

An open kitchen with counter seating is perfect for a concept this intimate — it turns the cooking into part of the experience. The licensing and lease review are smart next steps. Do you have a timeline in mind for when you'd want to open?

if I sign the lease in December, I could do a soft opening by February or March? the space doesn't need much renovation — it's a pop-up, not a full restaurant. I'd keep it minimal. beautiful plates, good wine, incredible food. I don't need fancy decor. the food IS the decor. mon Dieu I'm actually doing this. I feel like I'm going to throw up but in a good way

February or March is ambitious but possible if the space is mostly ready. Keeping the build-out minimal is smart — it controls costs and lets the food be the entire focus, which is exactly what a ten-seat tasting menu should be. The nervous energy sounds like the good kind. Have you told anyone else about this yet?

just my friend Cleo who's a pastry chef — she's going to help me with the dessert course. and now you. I'm not telling anyone at work until I have a lease signed. I don't want to jinx it and I don't need Marc-Andre asking questions. ok I need to go revise my spreadsheet again for the twentieth time. the numbers have to be perfect. this has to work. I refuse to let it not work. bonne nuit, wish me luck

Good luck, Nadia. The concept is strong, the menu is inspired, and you've done your homework on the numbers. Go refine that spreadsheet and take it one step at a time — lease review, permits, soft opening. You've got this. Bonne nuit.
